message.content:
You are verifier #2, independent of all other verifiers. Do NOT confirm findings optimistically — only confirm if you can independently verify the issue exists in the actual code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay.

Finding F-016: "corpus_fingerprint() materialises entire corpus into RAM with no streaming path"
File: src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py (line 44)
Description: corpus_fingerprint() sorts corpus.units and relations into lists, builds O(U) dicts, then passes to json.dumps(). For 100k-unit corpus doubles peak memory. Also called twice per run_study.
PoC: For 100k units, two calls happen: pipeline.py:365 and determinism.py:115 inside the builder. Full corpus JSON built in RAM twice.

Instructions:
1. Read the actual file at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/baseline/determinism.py
2. Look at the code at line 44 and surrounding context
3. Independently verify whether the described problem EXISTS in the current code
4. Check if the PoC is reproducible from the actual code as written
5. Consider whether this is already fixed (pass-1 #101-#122, pass-2 #124-#156 fixed many issues)
6. Return your independent verdict

Confirmed = true ONLY if: (a) the code problem exists as described, (b) the PoC is reproducible from current code, (c) it is a genuine production-readiness concern. Set confirmed=false for speculative issues, issues already fixed, or issues where the PoC does not match what the code actually does.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	"""Determinism & reproducibility harness for the baseline (ADR-0001, METHODOLOGY.md §1, §7).
2	
3	A baseline is only a yardstick if the *same inputs reproduce the same baseline*. This module
4	makes that contract concrete and engine-level: it content-hashes inputs, derives seeds
5	deterministically from those hashes, records component versions, and stamps all of it into a
6	:class:`~assay_engine.baseline.toolkit.BaselineArtifact`'s ``determinism`` record — so a
7	hostile reviewer can confirm a baseline was produced exactly as claimed.
8	
9	It is dependency-free and pure. The heavy, *choice-bearing* builders (which embedding model,
10	which clustering algorithm, which graph construction) encode dataset/study decisions and are
11	supplied by a study's adapter as :class:`~assay_engine.baseline.toolkit.BaselineBuilder`
12	implementations — not prescribed by the engine (ADR-0002). This harness records *how* such a
13	builder ran so the result is reproducible regardless of which builder it was.
14	"""
15	
16	from __future__ import annotations
17	
18	import hashlib
19	import json
20	import sys
21	from dataclasses import dataclass
22	from typing import Any, Mapping
23	
24	from assay_engine import __version__ as _ENGINE_VERSION
25	
26	# The canonical hashing primitives moved to ``assay_engine._canonical`` (see note below); these
27	# imports re-export them at their historical home so existing call sites keep working.
28	from assay_engine._canonical import canonical_plain as _plain  # noqa: F401
29	from assay_engine._canonical import hash_bytes as hash_bytes  # noqa: F401  (re-export)
30	from assay_engine._canonical import hash_text
31	from assay_engine._canonical import hash_value as hash_value  # re-export (baseline public API)
32	from assay_engine._canonical import keytag as _keytag  # noqa: F401
33	from assay_engine._frozen import freeze_mapping
34	from assay_engine.baseline.toolkit import BaselineArtifact
35	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus
36	
37	# The canonical hashing primitives (``_plain``/``_keytag``/``hash_bytes``/``hash_text``/
38	# ``hash_value``) live in ``assay_engine._canonical`` so they sit below both ``baseline`` and
39	# ``methodology`` in the import graph (audit #B1/#B2/#B5 originally landed here; hoisted so
40	# pre-registration can content-bind a hypothesis with the *same* type-faithful digest). They are
41	# re-exported above for back-compat.
42	
43	
44	def corpus_fingerprint(corpus: Corpus) -> str:
45	    """Deterministic content hash of a corpus (order-independent across units/relations)."""
46	    units = [
47	        {"id": u.unit_id, "text": u.text, "attrs": _plain(u.attributes)}
48	        for u in sorted(corpus.units, key=lambda u: u.unit_id)
49	    ]
50	    relations = [
51	        {"s": r.source_id, "t": r.target_id, "k": r.kind, "attrs": _plain(r.attributes)}
52	        for r in sorted(corpus.relations, key=lambda r: (r.source_id, r.target_id, r.kind))
53	    ]
54	    payload = {"units": units, "relations": relations, "metadata": _plain(corpus.metadata)}
55	    return hash_text(json.dumps(payload, sort_keys=True, allow_nan=False))
56	
57	
58	def stable_seed(*parts: str, bits: int = 32) -> int:
59	    """A deterministic non-negative integer seed derived from ``parts``.
60	
61	    Derived from a hash so the same inputs always yield the same seed — reproducible without a
62	    hard-coded magic number, and distinct inputs get distinct seeds.
63	    """
64	    digest = hashlib.sha256("\x1f".join(parts).encode("utf-8")).hexdigest()
65	    return int(digest, 16) % (1 << bits)
66	
67	
68	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
69	class DeterminismRecord:
70	    """The reproducibility provenance stamped onto a baseline (ADR-0001)."""
71	
72	    seed: int
73	    input_hashes: Mapping[str, str]
74	    component_versions: Mapping[str, str]
75	
76	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
77	        object.__setattr__(self, "input_hashes", freeze_mapping(self.input_hashes))
78	        object.__setattr__(self, "component_versions", freeze_mapping(self.component_versions))
79	
80	    def as_dict(self) -> dict[str, Any]:
81	        return {
82	            "seed": self.seed,
83	            "input_hashes": dict(self.input_hashes),
84	            "component_versions": dict(self.component_versions),
85	        }
86	
87	
88	def build_baseline_artifact(
89	    corpus: Corpus,
90	    contents: Mapping[str, Any],
91	    *,
92	    component_versions: Mapping[str, str] | None = None,
93	    extra_inputs: Mapping[str, Any] | None = None,
94	    seed: int | None = None,
95	    corpus_hash: str | None = None,
96	    trust_corpus_hash: bool = False,
97	) -> BaselineArtifact:
98	    """Assemble a :class:`BaselineArtifact` with a complete determinism record.
99	
100	    ``contents`` is the builder's output (embeddings, similarity graph, clusters, …).
101	    ``component_versions`` records the builder + any model/library versions (the engine and
102	    Python versions are added automatically). ``extra_inputs`` are additional inputs to hash
103	    (e.g. config). ``seed``, if omitted, is derived deterministically from the corpus + inputs
104	    so it is reproducible.
105	
106	    ``corpus_hash`` lets a caller pass an already-computed :func:`corpus_fingerprint` (#119). By
107	    default it is **verified** against the corpus and a mismatch raises ``ValueError`` — a
108	    stale/typo'd hash would otherwise produce a determinism record whose fingerprint and derived
109	    seed describe a *different* corpus than the one passed, silently defeating the reproducibility
110	    guarantee this harness exists to provide (#155). Pass ``trust_corpus_hash=True`` to skip the
111	    recompute (the #119 optimization) only when the caller computed the hash from this exact
112	    corpus and accepts integrity responsibility.
113	    """
114	    if corpus_hash is None:
115	        corpus_hash = corpus_fingerprint(corpus)
116	    elif not trust_corpus_hash:
117	        # Verify the supplied hash binds the corpus actually passed (default-safe, #155).
118	        actual = corpus_fingerprint(corpus)
119	        if corpus_hash != actual:
120	            raise ValueError(
121	                f"corpus_hash {corpus_hash!r} does not match the corpus "
122	                f"(corpus_fingerprint={actual!r}) — the determinism record would bind the wrong "
123	                "data; pass trust_corpus_hash=True only if you accept integrity responsibility"
124	            )
125	    if extra_inputs and "corpus" in extra_inputs:
126	        raise ValueError("'corpus' is a reserved input-hash key — rename the extra input (#B7)")
127	    input_hashes = {"corpus": corpus_hash}
128	    for name, value in (extra_inputs or {}).items():
129	        input_hashes[name] = hash_value(value)
130	
131	    versions = {"engine": _ENGINE_VERSION, "python": sys.version.split()[0]}
132	    versions.update(component_versions or {})
133	
134	    if seed is None:
135	        # Derive from all input hashes (the corpus hash is already among them under "corpus").
136	        seed = stable_seed(*(input_hashes[k] for k in sorted(input_hashes)))
137	
138	    record = DeterminismRecord(seed=seed, input_hashes=input_hashes, component_versions=versions)
139	    return BaselineArtifact(
140	        corpus_fingerprint=corpus_hash, contents=contents, determinism=record.as_dict()
141	    )
142

content.text:
Now I have enough information to make my assessment. Let me analyze what I found:

1. `corpus_fingerprint()` at line 44 does materialize the entire corpus into RAM: it creates a list of dicts for all units (sorted), a list of dicts for all relations, builds a payload dict, then calls `json.dumps()`. This is O(U) memory in addition to the corpus itself.

2. At pipeline.py:365, `corpus_fingerprint(corpus)` is called explicitly and the result stored as `cfp`.

3. Later, when builders call `build_baseline_artifact()`, by default (without `trust_corpus_hash=True` and without passing `corpus_hash=cfp`), `build_baseline_artifact` will call `corpus_fingerprint(corpus)` again at line 115, OR if `corpus_hash` is passed without `trust_corpus_hash=True`, it will call it again at line 118 to verify.

4. However, the pipeline.py stores `cfp` at line 365 and passes it at line 373 only to `trail.record()` for logging. Whether it also passes `cfp` to the builder's `build_baseline_artifact` call depends on the builder implementation, not the pipeline itself.

5. Looking at `reference_study.py` (line 53-57), `build_baseline_artifact` is called WITHOUT passing `corpus_hash` — so it will always recompute `corpus_fingerprint(corpus)` at line 115. This means the fingerprint IS computed twice: once in pipeline.py:365, and once inside `build_baseline_artifact` at determinism.py:115.

The finding is confirmed: the function materializes the entire corpus into RAM (no streaming), and it is called twice per run (once in pipeline.py:365, and once inside `build_baseline_artifact` at determinism.py:115 since callers like `reference_study.py` don't pass `corpus_hash`).

input.reasoning:
The code at determinism.py:44-55 confirms the memory pattern: corpus_fingerprint() builds a list of dicts for every unit (sorted), a list of dicts for every relation, assembles a payload dict, and calls json.dumps() — the entire corpus content is serialized into RAM at once with no streaming path. For 100k units this doubles peak memory.

The double-call PoC is also confirmed. pipeline.py:365 calls corpus_fingerprint(corpus) and stores the result as cfp, which is passed only to trail.record() for audit logging (line 373). The cfp value is NOT passed to the builder's build_baseline_artifact call. Callers such as tests/reference_study.py:53-57 call build_baseline_artifact(corpus, ...) without passing corpus_hash, so build_baseline_artifact unconditionally calls corpus_fingerprint(corpus) again at determinism.py:115. This is two full materializations per run_study.

The trust_corpus_hash=True escape hatch exists (determinism.py:96,116) and the docstring at line 110-112 documents the optimization, but callers in the actual adapter implementations do not use it — so the double-call is the real-world code path, not a theoretical one.
